feedback.body:
## Risk and Safety Evaluation

**[Overall PR] ⚠️ Risk Assessment: 🟡 MEDIUM**

**Risk Classification Rationale:**

This PR scores medium risk due to SDK coordination requirements, despite excellent code quality:

**Low-risk factors (positive):**
- ✅ **Pattern conformance**: Improves conformance by adopting the standard `SdkSectionPage` pattern used across other settings pages
- ✅ **Security sensitivity**: No security-sensitive code touched
- ✅ **Infrastructure dependencies**: No new external dependencies
- ✅ **Blast radius**: Change is isolated to the verification settings UI component
- ✅ **Code quality**: Reduces complexity from ~100 lines to ~50 lines by eliminating manual state management

**Medium-risk factors (concerns):**
- ⚠️ **SDK coordination**: Frontend mock updated with "critical" prominence for `confirmation_mode`, but actual SDK may not have this change yet
- ⚠️ **Deployment timing**: If deployed before SDK release, `confirmation_mode` may temporarily move from Basic to Advanced view in production
- ⚠️ **Behavior dependency**: Old code manually rendered `confirmation_mode` in header (always visible); new code relies on schema prominence

**Recommendation:**

This refactor demonstrates excellent engineering (simplification through better abstractions), but deployment sequence matters:

1. **Preferred**: Deploy this PR **after** the SDK release that includes the `confirmation_mode` prominence change
2. **Alternative**: Accept temporary UX regression where `confirmation_mode` appears in Advanced view until SDK is updated

The PR description acknowledges this ("will become visible in Basic once the next SDK release ships"), suggesting the team is aware of the coordination requirement.

**Suggested focus areas for human reviewer:**
- Confirm deployment sequence relative to SDK release
- Verify that temporary UX regression (if any) is acceptable to product stakeholders
- Validate that no other settings depend on the old `VERIFICATION_SCHEMA_EXCLUDE_KEYS` pattern

linked_prs.title: Filter out ConversationStateUpdateEvent from shared-events endpoints
bugfix(enterprise): Remove shared conversation enumerator endpoints

solution.text:
PR #13976: bugfix(enterprise): Remove shared conversation enumerator endpoints

  - [x] A human has tested these changes.

  ---

  ## Why

  `GET /api/shared-conversations/search` and `/count` were unauthenticated and required no `conversation_id`, which let anyone list or count every shared conversation in the system. Public share links of the form `/shared/conversations/<id>` need to keep working without auth, but enumerating all shares is not something we intend to expose.

  ## Summary

  - Removes the `/search` and `/count` route handlers from `shared_conversation_router.py`, leaving only the per-ID batch lookup the public viewer needs.
  - Drops the now-dead service methods (`search_shared_conversation_info`, `count_shared_conversation_info`, the `_apply_filters` helper, and the long-stale `_public_select`
  helper) plus the `SharedConversationPage` and `SharedConversationSortOrder` models that existed solely to support them, along with the corresponding tests.

  ## Issue Number

https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/13972

  ## How to Test

  Verified against a deployed build of this branch on a test instance using a known shared conversation id `d28670c95c224d9481d55f165fb49192`.

  **1. Enumeration endpoints no longer served by the API** — requests fall through to the React SPA (text/html), not the FastAPI router:
```
  $ curl -sS -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{content_type}\n"
      "$HOST/api/shared-conversations/search?limit=5"
  200 text/html; charset=utf-8
```

```
  $ curl -sS -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{content_type}\n"
      "$HOST/api/shared-conversations/count"
  200 text/html; charset=utf-8
```

  (Body in each case is `<!DOCTYPE html>…<title>OpenHands</title>…`, i.e. the SPA shell, not a JSON payload.)

  **2. Per-ID batch lookup still works** — this is what the public share-link viewer depends on:
```
  $ curl -sS "$HOST/api/shared-conversations?ids=d28670c95c224d9481d55f165fb49192"
  [{"id":"d28670c95c224d9481d55f165fb49192",
    "created_by_user_id":"0605dd60-13c3-4049-a165-ff691ab7ab89",
    "sandbox_id":"4SafaZuAKu4fBnalQE2D71",
    "title":"✨ Do a Flip",
    "llm_model":"anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
    ...}]
```

  **3. Event retrieval is scoped to a known conversation id** — succeeds with the id, rejects without it:
```
  $ curl -sS -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n"
      "$HOST/api/shared-events/search?conversation_id=d28670c95c224d9481d55f165fb49192&limit=3"
  200   # ~64 KB of events for the one conversation
```

```
  $ curl -sS "$HOST/api/shared-events/search?limit=5"
  {"detail":[{"type":"missing","loc":["query","conversation_id"],
              "msg":"Field required","input":null}]}
  HTTP 422
```

  ## Video/Screenshots

  ## Type

  - [x] Bug fix
  - [ ] Feature
  - [ ] Refactor
  - [ ] Breaking change
  - [ ] Docs / chore

  ## Notes

---

To run this PR locally, use the following command:

GUI with Docker:
```
docker run -it --rm   -p 3000:3000   -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock   --add-host host.docker.internal:host-gateway   -e SANDBOX_RUNTIME_CONTAINER_IMAGE=docker.openhands.dev/openhands/runtime:91b7503-nikolaik   --name openhands-app-91b7503   docker.openhands.dev/openhands/openhands:91b7503
```

Changed files: enterprise/server/sharing/shared_conversation_info_service.py, enterprise/server/sharing/shared_conversation_models.py, enterprise/server/sharing/shared_conversation_router.py, enterprise/server/sharing/sql_shared_conversation_info_service.py, enterprise/tests/unit/test_sharing/test_shared_conversation_models.py, enterprise/tests/unit/test_sharing/test_sharing_shared_conversation_info_service.py

**[RISK ASSESSMENT]**
⚠️ Risk Assessment: 🟢 **LOW**

This fixes a real security vulnerability (unauthenticated enumeration) with a clean, complete implementation. The per-ID batch lookup needed for public share links remains functional. All related code (routes, service methods, models, tests) properly removed. Manual testing confirms the fix works as intended.

**VERDICT:** ✅ **Worth merging** - Solid security fix with thorough cleanup.

linked_prs.title: fix(settings): align MCP settings layout with other settings pages

solution.text:
PR #13996: fix(settings): align MCP settings layout with other settings pages

Fixes #13982

## Problem
The MCP settings page had `max-w-[1000px] mx-auto px-6` on its outer container, which caused the content to be centered with extra padding rather than left-aligned. This made the MCP settings visually inconsistent with other settings pages (e.g., Secrets, Git settings) that start content from the left edge.

## Solution
Removed `max-w-[1000px]`, `mx-auto`, and `px-6` from the list view wrapper in `mcp-settings.tsx`. The content now aligns to the left, consistent with the rest of the settings pages which use the same layout provided by `SettingsLayout`.

## Testing
- Verified the MCP settings list view now starts from the left edge, matching `secrets-settings` and other settings pages.
- No functional behavior was changed; only the layout class was adjusted.

Changed files: frontend/src/routes/mcp-settings.tsx

solution.text:
PR #14013: Hide All toggle on SaaS LLM settings

<!-- Keep this PR as draft until it is ready for review. -->

<!-- AI/LLM agents: be concise and specific. Do not check the box below. -->

- [x] A human has tested these changes.

HUMAN: I have checked this on saas and the basic+advanced buttons show up, and all is hidden. Save seems to work fine.

---

## Why

The LLM settings page currently exposes the `All` view in commercial deployments, which surfaces low-level configuration that should not be shown in SaaS/OHE.

## Summary

- hide the LLM settings `All` view for `app_mode === "saas"`
- normalize the LLM page away from the `all` tier when it is disabled
- add route-level frontend coverage to keep `Advanced` visible in SaaS while hiding `All`

## Issue Number

Closes #13986.

## How to Test

1. `cd frontend && npm install`
2. `cd frontend && npm run test -- __tests__/routes/llm-settings.test.tsx`
3. `cd frontend && npm run lint:fix`
4. `cd frontend && npm run build`
5. In OSS/local mock mode, confirm the LLM page shows `Basic`, `Advanced`, and `All` when the schema includes both major and minor fields.
6. In SaaS mock mode, confirm the LLM page still shows `Basic` and `Advanced`, but hides `All`.

feedback.author: xdien
feedback.body:
**Instead of completely hiding the "All" toggle, consider implementing role-based permissions or making it configurable via environment variables (`.env`).**

This approach would provide more flexibility:
- **Role-based access**: Allow administrators to see and modify advanced LLM settings while regular users see a simplified view
- **Environment configuration**: Use an env variable like `SHOW_ADVANCED_LLM_SETTINGS=true/false` to control visibility based on deployment needs

This way, the functionality remains available for power users or self-hosted deployments while keeping the SaaS experience clean and simple.

feedback.author: neubig
feedback.body:
The "Advanced" tab disappeared because of PR #14033 ("Remove agent from the LLM settings page"), which was merged into `main` and then into this branch.

**Root cause**: PR #14033 changed `sectionKeys` from `["llm", "general"]` to `["llm"]`. The `general` section contained the `agent` field with `prominence: "major"`, which was the only reason `hasAdvancedSettings()` returned `true` for the LLM page. The `llm` section itself has **zero** `major` prominence fields — its non-critical fields (timeout, temperature, retries, AWS creds, etc.) are all `minor`. So once the `general` section was dropped, Advanced has nothing to show.

Here is the full prominence breakdown from the real SDK schema (`AgentSettings.export_schema()`):

| Section | Field | Prominence |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| `llm` | `llm.model`, `llm.api_key`, `llm.base_url` | **critical** |
| `llm` | all other fields (timeout, temperature, AWS creds, retries, etc.) | **minor** |
| `general` *(removed by #14033)* | `agent`, `tools` | **major** ← was driving the Advanced tab |

This means the three-tier view (Basic → Advanced → All) is effectively broken for the LLM page now — it jumps from Basic (critical only) straight to All (critical + minor), skipping Advanced entirely because there are no major LLM fields.

The fix likely needs to happen upstream in the SDK by re-classifying some LLM fields (e.g. `llm.temperature`, `llm.timeout`, `llm.num_retries`) as `major` instead of `minor`, so the Advanced tier has meaningful content again. Alternatively the frontend could be updated to collapse to a two-tier (Basic/All) toggle when no major fields exist, but that would be a design change.

_This comment was generated by an AI assistant (OpenHands)._

feedback.author: openhands-ai
feedback.body:
## Summary

The user's question was: **"I don't see the 'Advanced' tab in LLM settings anymore, maybe because I merged main. Was there a PR that removed the advanced tab?"**

### Answer

**Yes — PR #14033 ("Remove agent from the LLM settings page")** is the cause. It was merged into `main` and then into this branch.

**Root cause:** PR #14033 changed `sectionKeys` in `llm-settings.tsx` from `["llm", "general"]` to `["llm"]`. The `general` section contained the `agent` and `tools` fields with `prominence: "major"`, which were the **only** fields that caused `hasAdvancedSettings()` to return `true` for the LLM page. The `llm` section itself has **zero** `major` prominence fields — all its non-critical fields (timeout, temperature, retries, AWS credentials, etc.) are classified as `minor` in the SDK schema (`openhands-sdk 1.17.0`).

**Result:** The three-tier view (Basic → Advanced → All) is now effectively broken for the LLM page. It jumps from Basic (critical fields only) straight to All (critical + minor), with nothing for Advanced to show.

**Possible fixes:**
1. **SDK-side**: Re-classify some LLM fields (e.g. `llm.temperature`, `llm.timeout`, `llm.num_retries`) from `minor` to `major` so the Advanced tier has content.
2. **Frontend-side**: Collapse to a two-tier (Basic/All) toggle when no `major` fields exist in the filtered schema.

I posted this analysis as a comment on [PR #14013](https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/pull/14013#issuecomment-4284854850). No code changes were made — this was purely an investigative/diagnostic response to the reviewer's question.

problem:
VS Code tab shows "Bad Gateway" — stale URL after new runtime provisioned on conversation resume

When a conversation is resumed after its runtime was auto-paused, a **new runtime is provisioned**, but the VS Code tab still loads the URL from the **old runtime**, resulting in a "Bad Gateway" error.

## Root Cause

The VS Code URL becomes stale because the frontend TanStack Query cache is not invalidated when a sandbox is resumed with a new runtime:

1. Conversation starts → sandbox gets **runtime A** → VS Code URL = `https://runtimeA.prod-runtime.all-hands.dev`
2. Runtime A idles out and is auto-paused (K8s Service + Ingress deleted, deployment scaled to 0)
3. User resumes the conversation → **new runtime B** is provisioned
4. The frontend still has the cached VS Code URL for **runtime A** (via TanStack Query `useBatchSandboxes` / `useUnifiedVSCodeUrl`)
5. The iframe loads the old URL → Traefik has no backend for it → **"Bad Gateway"**

The infra layer (runtime-API) works correctly — it provisions the new runtime and returns the correct URL when queried. The issue is that the frontend does not refetch sandbox data after resume.

## Relevant Code

- **`frontend/src/hooks/query/use-unified-vscode-url.ts`** — gets VS Code URL from `sandbox.exposed_urls` via `useBatchSandboxes`. The query key includes the `sandbox` object, but if the sandbox query cache is not invalidated on resume, it keeps returning stale data.
- **`frontend/src/hooks/query/use-batch-sandboxes.ts`** — fetches sandbox data including `exposed_urls`.
- The **resume mutation** (`POST /api/v1/sandboxes/{id}/resume`) does not appear to invalidate the sandbox or VS Code URL query caches.

## Datadog Evidence (conversation `5961d9222af04b0fbc27ccfe789fea7d`)

- **Sandbox**: `5mIr7g6soMCiTNkUPZaxv4`
- **Original runtime**: `tjmlvsnfikwowklh` (VS Code URL: `https://tjmlvsnfikwowklh.prod-runtime.all-hands.dev`)

| Time (UTC) | Event |
|---|---|
| 15:29:11 | Sandbox started with runtime `tjmlvsnfikwowklh` |
| 16:07:58 | Runtime **paused** (idle 1208s) — K8s Service & Ingress deleted |
| 16:51:00 | Sandbox resumed → new runtime provisioned |
| 17:42:36 | Runtime **paused again** (idle 1360s) — Service & Ingress deleted |
| 18:16:27 | Sandbox resumed again |

After each resume, the VS Code tab continued to show "Bad Gateway" because it was loading the old runtime URL.

## Impact

Users see raw "Bad Gateway" text in the VS Code tab after resuming a conversation. The agent and terminal work fine (using the new runtime), but VS Code is broken with no way to recover from the UI.

## Suggested Fix

1. **Invalidate sandbox cache on resume** — The resume mutation should call `queryClient.invalidateQueries` for the sandbox and VS Code URL query keys, forcing a refetch that picks up the new runtime's URL.

2. **Detect iframe load failure** — Add error detection to the VS Code iframe (e.g., polling runtime status or listening for load errors) and trigger a sandbox data refetch + iframe reload when the URL appears stale.

---

_This issue was created by an AI assistant (OpenHands) on behalf of the user, based on a Datadog investigation._

linked_prs.title: fix: invalidate sandbox and VS Code URL caches on conversation resume

solution.text:
PR #13988: fix: invalidate sandbox and VS Code URL caches on conversation resume

- [ ] A human has tested these changes.

---

## Why

When a conversation is resumed after its runtime was auto-paused, a **new runtime** is provisioned but the VS Code tab still loads the URL from the **old runtime**, resulting in a "Bad Gateway" error. This happens because the TanStack Query cache for sandbox data and VS Code URLs is not invalidated when the resume mutation settles.

## Summary

- Invalidate `["sandboxes"]` and `["unified", "vscode_url"]` query caches in both `invalidateConversationQueries` (used by `useUnifiedResumeConversationSandbox`) and `useV1ResumeConversation`'s `onSettled` callback
- Added tests for both resume hooks verifying sandbox and VS Code URL cache invalidation

## Issue Number

Fixes #13987

## How to Test

1. Start a conversation, open the VS Code tab (should work)
2. Wait for the runtime to idle out and get paused
3. Resume the conversation
4. The VS Code tab should load the new runtime's URL instead of showing "Bad Gateway"

Alternatively, run the new unit tests:
```bash
cd frontend && npx vitest run __tests__/hooks/mutation/use-unified-resume-conversation-sandbox.test.tsx __tests__/hooks/mutation/use-v1-resume-conversation.test.tsx
```

## Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Breaking change
- [ ] Docs / chore

## Notes

The fix invalidates all sandbox-related caches (using the partial key `["sandboxes"]`) rather than specific sandbox IDs, since the mutation doesn't have direct access to the sandbox ID. TanStack Query's partial key matching ensures all sandbox batch queries are invalidated. This also fixes the same stale-URL issue for worker/active host URLs, which depend on the same sandbox data.

@xingyaoww can click here to [continue refining the PR](https://app.all-hands.dev/conversations/9a5d0557-49fb-43b4-8d03-0a5ed8e952d1)


problem:
Render ACPToolCallEvent in the conversation viewer

When an ACP agent (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI via the Agent Client Protocol) runs tools during a conversation, the agent-server emits `ACPToolCallEvent`s to the event stream. The frontend has no renderer for this event type, so the user sees only the final `MessageEvent` — no visibility into the tool calls that produced the answer.

Verified with an end-to-end ACP run: 12 events total, including 3 \`ACPToolCallEvent\`s each carrying \`title\`, \`tool_kind\`, \`status\`, \`raw_input\`, \`raw_output\`. Grep of \`frontend/src\` returns **zero matches** for \`ACPToolCall\`, so every one of them is silently dropped from the UI.

## Proposal

Add a renderer mapping \`ACPToolCallEvent\` → a Claude-Code-style tool card:

* **Title** (e.g. \`gh pr diff 490 --repo OpenHands/evaluation\`).
* **Status pill** (in_progress / completed / failed).
* **Tool kind badge** (\`execute\`, \`edit\`, \`read\`, …).
* **Collapsed input** — \`raw_input\` rendered (prefer \`command\` for \`execute\`, \`path\`+\`content\` for \`edit\`, etc.).
* **Expandable output** — \`raw_output\` with a collapse-until-click control (matches the existing command-output component).
* **Error styling** when \`status == "failed"\` / \`is_error\` / \`ConversationErrorEvent\` with matching \`tool_call_id\` is present.

The existing \`ActionEvent\`/\`ObservationEvent\` pair renderer is a good visual template — just read different fields.

## Event shape

```json
{
  \"kind\": \"ACPToolCallEvent\",
  \"id\": \"03341143-0efa-49d6-9b05-42471466baec\",
  \"timestamp\": \"2026-04-16T19:32:29.828069\",
  \"source\": \"agent\",
  \"tool_call_id\": \"toolu_01QFDF9PtYBz67yj3SwzrthQ\",
  \"title\": \"gh pr diff 490 --repo OpenHands/evaluation\",
  \"tool_kind\": \"execute\",
  \"status\": \"completed\",
  \"raw_input\": {\"command\": \"gh pr diff 490 --repo OpenHands/evaluation\"},
  \"raw_output\": \"…tool output…\",
  \"content\": [ /* optional ACP content blocks */ ],
  \"is_error\": false
}
```

## Companion SDK issue

[AGE-1309](https://linear.app/all-hands-ai/issue/AGE-1309/acpagent-stream-tool-call-events-from-session-update-instead-of) **— ACPAgent: stream tool-call events from session_update instead of batching at turn end**

Today all ACP tool events arrive in one burst at the end of \`prompt()\`. The SDK issue fixes that so events are emitted as each tool call starts / progresses / completes. This frontend renderer is useful even with the current batched delivery; streaming is what makes the UX feel like Claude Code's own web UI.

## Context

Shipped via the ACP-in-GUI PRs:

* [OpenHands/OpenHands#13983](<https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/13983>) — \`feat(app-server): route ACP agents to the ACP conversation endpoint\`
* [OpenHands/software-agent-sdk#2861](<https://github.com/OpenHands/software-agent-sdk/issues/2861>) — \`feat(sdk): split AgentSettings into LLM/ACP discriminated union\`

solution.text:
PR #13994: feat(frontend): render ACPToolCallEvent in conversation viewer

## Summary

- ACP agents (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI via the Agent Client Protocol) emit `ACPToolCallEvent`s for every tool call they execute. The frontend had **zero renderers** for them, so every tool call was silently dropped and users saw only the final `MessageEvent`.
- ACP tool call cards now render through the **same pipeline as regular OpenHands observations** — `GenericEventMessageWrapper` → `getEventContent` → `SuccessIndicator`. No bespoke component, no custom pills, no custom collapse UI. The card shape, status icon, markdown body and truncation behavior all match the rest of the conversation view.
- The "kind" differentiator is a translated **`ACP · `** prefix in the title row (e.g. `ACP · Running <cmd>gh pr diff 490</cmd>`) via new `ACTION_MESSAGE$ACP_RUN` / `$ACP_EDIT` / `$ACP_READ` / `$ACP_FETCH` / `$ACP_TOOL` keys that mirror the existing `ACTION_MESSAGE$RUN` pattern with `<cmd>` / `<path>` component wrapping.
- Dedup by `tool_call_id` in the `uiEvents` layer so that, once OpenHands/software-agent-sdk#2866 starts streaming `in_progress → completed` updates, the same card updates in place rather than duplicating.

## Rendering — what maps to what

| Concern | Regular OpenHands observation | ACPToolCallEvent (this PR) |
|---|---|---|
| Card shape | `GenericEventMessageWrapper` + `GenericEventMessage` | **same** |
| Title | `ACTION_MESSAGE$RUN` = `Running <cmd>{command}</cmd>` | `ACTION_MESSAGE$ACP_RUN` = `ACP · Running <cmd>{title}</cmd>` (+ `$ACP_EDIT` / `$ACP_READ` / `$ACP_FETCH` / `$ACP_TOOL`) |
| Body | `getTerminalObservationContent` → markdown with `Command:` + `Output:` fenced blocks | `getACPToolCallContent` → **same** markdown shape; execute uses `Command:`, other kinds use `Input:` JSON block, `is_error` flips `Output:` to `**Error:**` |
| Status indicator | `SuccessIndicator` (check for success, clock for timeout, nothing for error/undefined) | **same component**, driven by `getACPToolCallResult` (`completed` → success, `failed`/`is_error` → error, `in_progress` → undefined ⇒ no icon, matching how `ActionEvent` renders before its `ObservationEvent` arrives) |
| Truncation | `MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH` (1000) with inline `...` | **same constant**, same marker |
| No-output fallback | `OBSERVATION$COMMAND_NO_OUTPUT` | **same key** |

## Files

### New

- `frontend/src/types/v1/core/events/acp-tool-call-event.ts` — `ACPToolCallEvent` type, `ACPToolKind` / `ACPToolCallStatus` unions, content block shape.
- `frontend/src/components/v1/chat/event-content-helpers/get-acp-tool-call-content.ts` — `getACPToolCallTitleKey` (tool_kind → i18n key) and `getACPToolCallContent` (markdown body).

### Modified

- `frontend/src/types/v1/core/events/index.ts`, `openhands-event.ts` — add `ACPToolCallEvent` to the V1 event union.
- `frontend/src/types/v1/type-guards.ts` — `isACPToolCallEvent` discriminator guard.
- `frontend/src/components/v1/chat/event-message.tsx` — dispatch ACP events through `GenericEventMessageWrapper` (identical to the observation path).
- `frontend/src/components/v1/chat/event-message-components/generic-event-message-wrapper.tsx` — pick up ACP status via the new `getACPToolCallResult` helper.
- `frontend/src/components/v1/chat/event-content-helpers/get-event-content.tsx` — route ACP events to `getACPToolCallContent` + title key.
- `frontend/src/components/v1/chat/event-content-helpers/get-observation-result.ts` — add `getACPToolCallResult` (lifecycle + error → `"success"` / `"error"` / `undefined`).
- `frontend/src/components/v1/chat/event-content-helpers/should-render-event.ts` — allowlist ACP events.
- `frontend/src/utils/handle-event-for-ui.ts` — dedup by `tool_call_id`; falls back to appending when no prior card exists.
- `frontend/src/i18n/translation.json` + regenerated `declaration.ts` — 5 new `ACTION_MESSAGE$ACP_*` keys, full 15-locale coverage (required by `check-translation-completeness` pre-commit hook).

## Test plan

- [x] `npm run typecheck` — clean.
- [x] `npx vitest run` — **1957 passing** (+7 over main), 0 regressions.
- [x] `npx eslint` — clean on all new / modified files.
- [x] **Content-helper unit tests** (`__tests__/components/v1/chat/event-content-helpers/get-acp-tool-call-content.test.ts`, 16 cases): title key mapping per `tool_kind` + null fallback; execute renders `Command:` + fenced `Output:`; non-execute renders `Input:` JSON block; `is_error` flips to `**Error:**`; empty output uses shared `OBSERVATION$COMMAND_NO_OUTPUT` fallback; truncation at `MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH`; structured JSON output serialised; `getACPToolCallResult` returns success / error / undefined for the three lifecycle states.
- [x] **Dispatch tests** (`__tests__/components/v1/chat/event-message-acp-tool-call.test.tsx`, 4 cases): ACP events route through the same wrapper; success check mark renders for completed; icon hidden while `in_progress`; details expand on click and contain both Command and Output text.
- [x] **Dedup tests** (`__tests__/utils/handle-event-for-ui.test.ts`): append on first ID, replace-in-place on same `tool_call_id`, leave other IDs untouched.
- [ ] Manual verify: start an ACP conversation, send a prompt that runs 2–3 tools, confirm cards render in order with `ACP · Running/Editing/Reading …` titles, success check marks appear on completion, and content expands with `Command:`/`Output:` blocks matching regular bash cards. _(Left for reviewer in a running dev environment — behavior is covered by unit tests.)_

## Notes for reviewers

- Net **-500 LOC** vs the first push — the refactor removes the standalone `ACPToolCallEventMessage` component, its custom i18n keys, its bespoke test file, and its custom pill / badge / collapse UI. The goal was deliberate: keep the visual language of the conversation viewer consistent rather than introduce a parallel "richer" card shape for ACP.
- The earlier design tradeoffs (what the richer card gave up) are documented in the commit history if you want to reason about whether to re-add any ACP-specific affordances (e.g. an explicit in-progress spinner pill) in a follow-up.

Closes #13991

```
docker run -it --rm   -p 3000:3000   -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock   --add-host host.docker.internal:host-gateway   --name openhands-app-bd76af4   docker.openhands.dev/openhands/openhands:bd76af4
```

problem:
Plumb user LLM credentials into ACP subprocess env (today only OH_AGENT_SERVER_ENV works)

The OpenHands GUI exposes ``llm.api_key`` and ``llm.base_url`` on the LLM settings page, and those are used correctly for the OpenHands (LLM) agent. But when the user picks the ACP variant, **those credentials never reach the ACP subprocess**.

The ACP subprocess (``claude-agent-acp`` / ``codex-acp`` / ``gemini-cli``) makes its own LLM calls directly and reads credentials from provider-specific env vars (``ANTHROPIC_API_KEY``/``ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL``, ``OPENAI_API_KEY``/``OPENAI_BASE_URL``, ``GEMINI_API_KEY``/``GEMINI_BASE_URL``). Today those env vars must be injected at agent-server launch time via ``OH_AGENT_SERVER_ENV``:

```bash
export OH_AGENT_SERVER_ENV='{
  "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY": "sk-…",
  "ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL": "https://llm-proxy.eval.all-hands.dev",
  "OPENAI_API_KEY": "sk-…",
  "OPENAI_BASE_URL": "https://llm-proxy.eval.all-hands.dev",
  "GEMINI_API_KEY": "sk-…",
  "GEMINI_BASE_URL": "https://llm-proxy.eval.all-hands.dev"
}'
```

That's a server-config escape hatch, not a UX. A user who configures a LiteLLM proxy in the GUI's LLM settings today can't use it for ACP without also restarting the backend with the right env vars.

## Current state

- **OpenHands (LLM) agent**: ``/settings`` writes ``llm.api_key`` / ``llm.base_url`` onto ``LLMAgentSettings.llm`` → used by ``create_agent()`` for real LLM calls. Works end-to-end. ✅
- **ACP agent**: ``/settings/acp-server`` + ``/settings/acp-model`` don't expose any credential field. ``ACPAgentSettings.llm`` exists in the SDK but is used only for cost/token attribution inside OpenHands; the subprocess never sees it. ``acp_env`` is exposed as a MINOR JSON field on the ACP Server page — technically a user could put ``{"ANTHROPIC_API_KEY": "…"}`` there manually, but it's raw-JSON and clearly not the intended UX. ❌

## Proposal

### Backend — translate saved LLM creds into provider env vars at conversation-start time

In ``openhands/app_server/app_conversation/live_status_app_conversation_service.py:_build_acp_start_conversation_request``, before we hand the ``ACPAgent`` to the agent-server, merge into ``acp_env`` the provider-specific env vars for the chosen ``acp_server``:

```python
acp_env: dict[str, str] = dict(acp_settings.acp_env)
llm = acp_settings.llm  # (or a shared AgentSettings.provider_credentials — see below)

if acp_settings.acp_server == "claude-code":
    if llm.api_key:
        acp_env.setdefault("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", llm.api_key.get_secret_value())
    if llm.base_url:
        acp_env.setdefault("ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL", llm.base_url)
elif acp_settings.acp_server == "codex":
    if llm.api_key:
        acp_env.setdefault("OPENAI_API_KEY", llm.api_key.get_secret_value())
    if llm.base_url:
        acp_env.setdefault("OPENAI_BASE_URL", llm.base_url)
elif acp_settings.acp_server == "gemini-cli":
    if llm.api_key:
        acp_env.setdefault("GEMINI_API_KEY", llm.api_key.get_secret_value())
    if llm.base_url:
        acp_env.setdefault("GEMINI_BASE_URL", llm.base_url)
# "custom" → user-supplied acp_env wins; we don't guess.
```

User-provided ``acp_env`` keys continue to take precedence (``setdefault``) so power users can still override.

### Frontend — surface a single "provider credentials" pair on the ACP pages

Options:

- **A. Duplicate ``llm.api_key`` + ``llm.base_url`` into the ACP Server (or Model) page.** Simplest, some duplication if a user also runs the LLM agent against the same proxy.
- **B. Add an ``AgentSettings.provider_credentials`` shared section** (api_key + base_url), used by both variants. Cleaner model, but slightly larger refactor (schema-side ``variant=None`` fields).

I'd go with **A** first; promote to **B** only if the duplication becomes painful.

### Security

- Treat the api key as ``SecretStr`` end-to-end; never log.
- The existing ``{'expose_secrets': True}`` context flag on model_dump already governs when the raw value leaves the backend.
- Env vars inside the sandbox container are as safe as the sandbox; matches how ``OH_AGENT_SERVER_ENV`` already works.

## Relation to existing work

- Built on top of ``feat/acp-discriminated-union-v3`` (OpenHands/OpenHands#13983 + OpenHands/software-agent-sdk#2861 + the three follow-ups: #2868, #2869, OpenHands/OpenHands#13993). None of those add credential plumbing.
- Closes the last UX gap between "configure ACP agent entirely via the GUI" and reality.

## Acceptance

- A user saves ``llm.api_key`` + ``llm.base_url`` in the GUI, picks ACP, starts a conversation, and the claude-agent-acp / codex-acp / gemini-cli subprocess hits the configured base URL with the configured key — no ``OH_AGENT_SERVER_ENV`` needed.
- User-supplied ``acp_env`` entries win over the auto-derived ones (override path).
- Clear visible feedback when credentials are missing (e.g. a small warning on the ACP pages if ``llm.api_key`` is unset).

solution.text:
PR #14321: feat(app-server): plumb llm.base_url into ACP subprocess env (#13999)

## Why

Closes #13999.

When a user configures both `llm.api_key` and `llm.base_url` in the UI (for example, pointing at a LiteLLM proxy), the ACP path today only plumbs `api_key` into the subprocess env. The `base_url` is silently dropped, so the ACP subprocess hits the provider default endpoint instead of the user-configured one — breaking the user's LiteLLM / proxy setup with no indication in the UI.

PR #14004 introduced `_acp_provider_env` and claimed in its description to plumb `*_BASE_URL` vars as well, but the merged implementation only emits `*_API_KEY`. This PR closes that gap.

## Summary

- Extend `LiveStatusAppConversationService._acp_provider_env` to also emit `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` / `OPENAI_BASE_URL` / `GEMINI_BASE_URL` when `llm.base_url` is set.
- Gate `*_BASE_URL` emission on `api_key` also being present, preserving PR #14004's security guarantee: "a provider-default `base_url` alone never clobbers an explicit `OH_AGENT_SERVER_ENV` passthrough".
- Mirror the existing SDK forward-compat pattern: prefer `acp_settings.base_url_env_var` when the SDK exposes it (per software-agent-sdk PR #2984 TODO), fall back to a per-server map for the current pinned SDK.
- Blank / whitespace-only `base_url` is treated as unset.
- Behavior for `custom` ACP servers is unchanged: no auto-plumbing, users continue to manage env via `acp_env`.

The end-to-end result: the acceptance criterion in #13999 holds — a user who saves `llm.api_key` + `llm.base_url` in the GUI, picks ACP, and starts a conversation will have the ACP subprocess reach the configured base URL with the configured key, no `OH_AGENT_SERVER_ENV` needed.

## Test plan

New unit tests in `TestAcpProviderEnv`:

- `test_claude_code_with_base_url_plumbs_both` — both vars emitted for `claude-code`.
- `test_codex_with_base_url_plumbs_both` — both vars emitted for `codex`.
- `test_gemini_with_base_url_plumbs_both` — both vars emitted for `gemini-cli`.
- `test_custom_server_with_base_url_returns_empty` — `custom` still returns `{}`.
- `test_blank_base_url_not_emitted` — whitespace-only `base_url` does not emit a `*_BASE_URL` entry.

The existing 6 tests (which did not set `base_url`) continue to pass unchanged.

Test command run locally:

```
.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/app_server/test_live_status_app_conversation_service.py -q
# 123 passed in 5.16s

.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/app_server/ -q
# 1092 passed in 33.38s
```

Ruff format + lint on the two changed files: clean (no new errors introduced; pre-existing ASYNC240 / I001 findings on the surrounding codebase are unchanged).

## AI assistance

AI assistance was used while investigating the gap between #13999's acceptance criteria and the merged #14004 implementation, and while drafting the test cases. A human author reviewed every changed line, ran the test suite locally, and is accountable for the change end-to-end.

- Why this isn't a duplicate: No open PR currently targets #13999 (only #14004 which is merged but incomplete on `base_url`).
- Tests run and results: see Test plan above (123 passed in file, 1092 passed in app_server tree).

Changed files: openhands/app_server/app_conversation/live_status_app_conversation_service.py, tests/unit/app_server/test_live_status_app_conversation_service.py

feedback.author: simonrosenberg
feedback.body: @openhands Is this issue still valid? I believe it might have been fixed by a previous PR. Investigate

feedback.author: simonrosenberg
feedback.body:
## Investigation Summary

After investigating the codebase, I found that **this issue is NOT yet fixed in the main branch**, but **PR #14004** addresses exactly this request.

### Current State (main branch)
- The `live_status_app_conversation_service.py` file does not have any ACP-specific handling
- There is no `_build_acp_start_conversation_request` method
- LLM credentials (`llm.api_key`, `llm.base_url`) are not translated into provider-specific env vars for ACP subprocesses

### The Fix (PR #14004)
PR #14004 on the `feat/acp-integration-layer` branch implements the proposed solution by:

1. **Adding a `_acp_provider_env()` method** that translates UI-saved LLM credentials into provider-specific environment variables:
   - `claude-code` → `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` / `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`
   - `codex` → `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_BASE_URL`
   - `gemini-cli` → `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_BASE_URL`

2. **Adding a `_build_acp_start_conversation_request()` method** that:
   - Detects when `AgentSettings` is an `ACPAgentSettings`
   - Merges derived credentials into `acp_env` (user-supplied entries take precedence)
   - Routes to `/api/acp/conversations` instead of `/api/conversations`

3. **Security considerations** are properly handled:
   - Credentials are gated on `api_key` being set (base URL alone won"t trigger plumbing)
   - `SecretStr` is handled properly via `get_secret_value()`
   - User-supplied `acp_env` entries override auto-derived ones

### Recommendation
This issue should be **closed as resolved once PR #14004 is merged**. The PR fully implements the acceptance criteria specified in this issue.

---

problem:
Add secrets field to AppConversationStartRequest for direct API secret passing

Add a `secrets` field to `AppConversationStartRequest` to allow API callers to pass secrets directly when starting a conversation with a plugin. This enables external integrations to provide secrets at conversation start time without requiring them to be pre-stored in the database.

## Background

Currently, secrets flow to the agent's `secret_registry` through:
1. Database-stored custom secrets (`StoredCustomSecrets` table)
2. Git provider tokens (GITHUB_TOKEN, GITLAB_TOKEN, etc.)

There is no way to pass secrets directly via the API when starting a conversation. This is needed for:
- Plugin-specific secrets that are provided at invocation time
- Integration scenarios where secrets are managed externally
- Temporary/session-scoped secrets that should not be persisted

## Proposed Changes

1. Add `secrets` field to `AppConversationStartRequest` model
2. Merge API-provided secrets with existing secrets in `_build_start_conversation_request_for_user()`
3. Update callers to pass secrets through the service layer

## Files to Modify

- `openhands/app_server/app_conversation/app_conversation_models.py`
- `openhands/app_server/app_conversation/live_status_app_conversation_service.py`
- `openhands/app_server/app_conversation/app_conversation_router.py`
- Tests

## Related Future Work (Separate Issues)

1. **Plugin Secret/Environment Requirements Specification (SDK)** - Allow plugins to declare required secrets in their manifest
2. **Pre-launch Validation of Plugin Requirements (OpenHands)** - Validate requirements are met before launching agent

---
*This issue was created by an AI assistant (OpenHands) on behalf of the user.*

solution.text:
PR #14009: feat: add secrets field to AppConversationStartRequest for direct API secret passing

## Summary
Adds a `secrets` field to `AppConversationStartRequest` allowing API callers to pass per-conversation secrets directly when starting a conversation.

## Changes
- Added optional `secrets: dict[str, str] | None` field to `AppConversationStartRequest` model
- Updated `_build_start_conversation_request_for_user()` to accept `api_secrets` parameter
- Updated conversation service to merge API-provided secrets with vault secrets (API secrets override vault secrets for same key)
- Added documentation warning about silent override behavior

## Testing
### E2E Test Results ✅
Tested with feature deployment `ohpr-14009-30.staging.all-hands.dev` using SDK PR #2873's agent-server image (`8a68c66-python`):


## Usage Examples

See the [oh-examples](https://github.com/jpshackelford/oh-examples) repository for complete working examples:

- **[per-conversation-secrets/](https://github.com/jpshackelford/oh-examples/tree/main/per-conversation-secrets)** - Full documentation and test scripts
- **[test_secrets_at_start.py](https://github.com/jpshackelford/oh-examples/blob/main/per-conversation-secrets/test_secrets_at_start.py)** - Test for the new `secrets` field (this PR)
- **[test_secrets.py](https://github.com/jpshackelford/oh-examples/blob/main/per-conversation-secrets/test_secrets.py)** - Test for injecting secrets after conversation start (original approach)

### Quick Example

```python
requests.post(
    f'{api_url}/v1/app-conversations',
    headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {api_key}'},
    json={
        'sandbox_id': sandbox_id,
        'initial_message': {'role': 'user', 'content': [{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Hello'}]},
        'secrets': {
            'GITHUB_TOKEN': 'ghp_...',
            'MY_API_KEY': 'sk-...',
        }
    }
)
```

## Related
- Closes #14007
- SDK PR: OpenHands/software-agent-sdk#2873 ✅ (merged in SDK v1.18.1)

## Before Merge ✅
- [x] Remove agent-server image tag override in `sandbox_spec_service.py` (now using `1.18.1-python`)
- [x] SDK PR #2873 merged and released as v1.18.1

feedback.author: jpshackelford
feedback.body:
## Implementation Details

### 1. Add `secrets` field to `AppConversationStartRequest`

**File:** `openhands/app_server/app_conversation/app_conversation_models.py`

```python
from openhands.sdk.secret import SecretSource, StaticSecret

class AppConversationStartRequest(OpenHandsModel):
    # ... existing fields ...
    
    secrets: dict[str, str | SecretSource] | None = Field(
        default=None,
        description=(
            'Additional secrets to pass to the agent. Keys are secret names, '
            'values can be plain strings (converted to StaticSecret) or SecretSource objects. '
            'These are merged with user-stored secrets, with API-provided secrets taking precedence.'
        )
    )
```

### 2. Modify `_build_start_conversation_request_for_user()` 

**File:** `openhands/app_server/app_conversation/live_status_app_conversation_service.py`

```python
async def _build_start_conversation_request_for_user(
    self,
    # ... existing params ...
    request_secrets: dict[str, str | SecretSource] | None = None,
):
    # Get git provider secrets (existing logic)
    secrets = await self._setup_secrets_for_git_providers(user)
    
    # Merge request secrets (API-provided secrets take precedence)
    if request_secrets:
        for name, value in request_secrets.items():
            if isinstance(value, str):
                secrets[name] = StaticSecret(value=SecretStr(value))
            else:
                secrets[name] = value
    
    # Continue with AgentContext creation...
```

## Verification

Secrets reaching the agent's `secret_registry` can be verified by:
1. SDK logging in `LocalConversation.update_secrets()`: `logger.info(f"Added {len(secrets)} secrets to conversation")`
2. Secrets appearing in agent's system prompt via `AgentContext.get_system_message_suffix()`
3. Runtime injection via `SecretRegistry.get_secrets_as_env_vars()` when bash commands reference secret keys

## Security Considerations

- API-provided secrets should be validated (key name format, value length limits)
- Secret names should be logged for audit trails (never values)
- Existing `SecretRegistry.mask_secrets_in_output()` handles masking in command output
- Consider rate limiting on secret-containing requests

feedback.author: all-hands-bot
feedback.body:
**Taste Rating**: 🟡 Acceptable - Solid implementation with minor documentation gaps.

**[TESTING GAPS]**
- [PR description] **No Evidence**: The PR description includes a code example but lacks concrete proof that the feature works end-to-end. Add an Evidence section showing: (1) an actual API request with the secrets field, (2) the response showing the conversation started successfully, (3) verification that secrets were available to the agent. For backend API changes like this, test output alone isn't sufficient - show the real HTTP request/response.

**[RISK ASSESSMENT]**
- [Overall PR] ⚠️ Risk Assessment: 🟡 MEDIUM

Rationale: Any change touching secrets handling is automatically medium risk minimum. This PR adds a new API field for passing secrets directly, with override semantics (API secrets take precedence over database/git provider secrets). The implementation follows existing security patterns (SecretStr, StaticSecret) and is backward compatible, preventing escalation to HIGH. However, the security sensitivity and API surface expansion warrant human review.

Key risk factors:
- Security-sensitive area (secrets handling)
- New API surface (request model field)
- Override behavior requires clear understanding by API consumers
- Moderate blast radius (conversation start flow)

Mitigating factors:
- Uses established security primitives
- Backward compatible (optional field)
- Well-tested unit tests covering merge logic and override behavior
- Follows existing code patterns

**Recommendation**: Merge with caution. Have a human reviewer validate: (1) the override semantics are appropriate for the use case, (2) API documentation clearly explains precedence rules, (3) there are no edge cases in secret merging that could leak or lose secrets.

**VERDICT:**
✅ **Worth merging**: Core logic is sound and well-tested. Address evidence gap before merge.

**KEY INSIGHT:**
The implementation correctly uses Pydantic's SecretStr for API transport and converts to StaticSecret for internal use, maintaining security invariants throughout the secret lifecycle.

feedback.author: tofarr
feedback.body: See my comment

feedback.author: all-hands-bot
feedback.body:
**Taste Rating**: 🟡 Acceptable - Solid validation design with critical SecretStr handling bug that must be fixed.

The overall architecture is well-designed with comprehensive validation logic, but there's a critical bug in how SecretStr objects are validated. The temporary agent-server image override also blocks merge.

**[RISK ASSESSMENT]**
- [Overall PR] ⚠️ Risk Assessment: 🟡 MEDIUM

This PR adds API-level secret injection, which is a sensitive feature area. The validation framework (blocked names, size limits, prefix checks) is well-designed and addresses the authorization concerns raised in reviews. However, the SecretStr validation bug means size limits are not enforced in practice, allowing potentially unbounded secret values to be stored and processed. The impact is limited (DoS via large payloads rather than direct security breach), but must be fixed before merge. The temporary agent-server image override is a procedural blocker, not a security issue.

feedback.author: jpshackelford
feedback.body:
## Code Path Analysis for PRs #14009 and #2873

I've traced the complete code path for both PRs and analyzed whether they form a complete solution.

### The Overall Problem
Passing secrets via the REST API at conversation start time and using them for MCP config variable expansion (`${VAR}` syntax in `.mcp.json` files).

---

### PR #14009 (OpenHands) - Complete Flow
**What it adds:**
1. `secrets: dict[str, SecretStr]` field to `AppConversationStartRequest`
2. Merges API-provided secrets in `_build_start_conversation_request_for_user()`

**Code path:**
```
API Request with secrets
    ↓
AppConversationStartRequest.secrets (dict[str, SecretStr])
    ↓
_build_start_conversation_request_for_user():
    secrets = await self._setup_secrets_for_git_providers(user)
    if api_secrets:
        secrets[name] = StaticSecret(value=value)  # NEW
    ↓
AgentContext(secrets=secrets)
    ↓
conv_settings.create_request(StartConversationRequest, agent=agent)
    ↓
_start_request_kwargs(): payload["secrets"] = agent.agent_context.secrets
    ↓
StartConversationRequest.secrets (dict[str, SecretSource])
```

---

### PR #2873 (software-agent-sdk) - Complete Flow
**What it adds:**
1. `SecretRegistry.get_all_secrets()` - exports all secrets as dict
2. `expand_mcp_variables(secrets=...)` - accepts secrets parameter
3. `expand_defaults` flag to control when defaults are applied
4. Plugin loading uses `expand_defaults=False` to preserve placeholders
5. `LocalConversation._ensure_plugins_loaded()` expands with secrets

**Code path:**
```
StartConversationRequest.secrets
    ↓
StoredConversation.secrets
    ↓
EventService.start() → LocalConversation(secrets=self.stored.secrets)
    ↓
LocalConversation.__init__():
    if secrets:
        self.update_secrets(secrets)  # Adds to SecretRegistry
    ↓
First run()/send_message() → _ensure_plugins_loaded():
    1. Load plugins (expand_defaults=False preserves ${VAR:-default})
    2. Merge MCP configs
    3. secrets = self._state.secret_registry.get_all_secrets()  # NEW
    4. expand_mcp_variables(merged_mcp, secrets=secrets, expand_defaults=True)  # NEW
    ↓
MCP config variables expanded with API secrets ✅
```

---

### ✅ Complete and Sufficient: Primary Use Case
**The two PRs together fully solve the primary use case:**
- User passes secrets at conversation start via API
- Secrets flow: OpenHands API → AgentContext → StartConversationRequest → SecretRegistry
- Plugin MCP configs correctly expand `${VAR}` and `${VAR:-default}` with API secrets
- Double-expansion bug is prevented (placeholders preserved until secrets available)

---

### ⚠️ Potential Gap: Dynamic Secret Injection

**Issue:** If a user adds secrets via `POST /api/conversations/{id}/secrets` AFTER plugins are already loaded, the MCP config will NOT be re-expanded.

**Scenario:**
1. Start conversation with plugin having `${MY_TOKEN}` in MCP config
2. First message triggers plugin loading - no `MY_TOKEN` secret → placeholder unchanged
3. User calls `POST /api/conversations/{id}/secrets` with `{"MY_TOKEN": "value"}`
4. Secret added to registry but MCP config NOT re-expanded

**Severity:** Low-Medium. The primary use case (secrets at conversation start) is fully supported. The dynamic injection use case may be rare for MCP configs.

**Potential future fix:** Add a method to re-expand MCP config after `update_secrets()`, or document that MCP-related secrets must be provided at conversation start.

---

### Summary

| Aspect | Status |
|--------|--------|
| Secrets passed at conversation start | ✅ Complete |
| MCP config `${VAR}` expansion | ✅ Complete |
| MCP config `${VAR:-default}` expansion | ✅ Complete |
| Double-expansion bug prevention | ✅ Complete |
| Type compatibility (StaticSecret → SecretSource) | ✅ Complete |
| Timing: secrets available before plugin loading | ✅ Complete |
| Dynamic secrets for MCP (post-start) | ⚠️ Gap |

**Conclusion:** These PRs form a **complete and sufficient solution** for the core problem of passing secrets via the API at conversation start time and using them for MCP config expansion. The only gap is a secondary use case (dynamic secret injection for MCP) which is not the focus of these PRs.

---
_This analysis was created by an AI assistant (OpenHands) on behalf of @jpshackelford._

> 3. Set the API URL (must include `/api` suffix): `export OH_API_URL="https://ohpr-14009-30.staging.all-hands.dev/api"`
> 4. Set the API key: `export OH_API_KEY="<provide-your-api-key>"`
> 5. Run both test scripts:
>    - `python test_secrets_at_start.py` (tests the NEW secrets-at-start feature)
>    - `python test_secrets.py` (tests the original secrets-after-start approach)
> 
> Report the test results including whether both tests pass and any relevant output.

feedback.body:
## 🧪 Follow-up: MCP Config Variable Expansion Test

This is a follow-up to the [previous test results](#issuecomment-4304201644), adding a critical end-to-end test for **MCP config variable expansion** from secrets passed at conversation start.

### Why This Test Matters

The previous tests verified that secrets are available as environment variables for bash commands. But the **key feature** of PR #14009 + SDK #2873 is enabling secrets to be expanded in **MCP config files** - allowing per-conversation MCP authentication tokens.

### Test Approach

**Script:** `test_mcp_secrets_at_start.py`

This test verifies the full MCP expansion flow:

1. **Start an MCP server** that expects a specific authentication token
2. **Start a conversation** with BOTH secrets AND a plugin in the same request:
   - `MCP_SERVER_URL` - URL of the MCP server
   - `MCP_SECRET_TOKEN` - Authentication token
3. **Plugin loads** with `.mcp.json` containing:
   ```json
   {
     "mcpServers": {
       "token-validator": {
         "url": "${MCP_SERVER_URL}/mcp",
         "headers": {
           "Authorization": "Bearer ${MCP_SECRET_TOKEN}"
         }
       }
     }
   }
   ```
4. **Variables expand** from secrets passed at conversation start
5. **Agent calls MCP tool** - server validates the token was correctly passed

### Test Results

| Test | Description | Result |
|------|-------------|--------|
| `test_mcp_secrets_at_start.py` | MCP config variable expansion from secrets at start | ✅ **PASSED** |

#### Test Output
```
======================================================================
  MCP SECRETS AT START TEST (PR #14009)
  Testing: secrets + plugin in AppConversationStartRequest
======================================================================

[12:24:31] API URL: https://ohpr-14009-30.staging.all-hands.dev/api
[12:24:31] MCP Server: https://work-1-nldsswnjknvddqsu.prod-runtime.all-hands.dev
[12:24:31] Verifying MCP server...
[12:24:31]   Server OK: {'status': 'ok', 'server': 'token-validator-mcp', 'validated_count': 0}
[12:24:31] Creating sandbox...
[12:25:04] Agent Server: https://uihloknquotcoerr.staging-runtime.all-hands.dev
[12:25:04] Starting conversation with secrets AND plugin...
[12:25:04]   Secrets: ['MCP_SERVER_URL', 'MCP_SECRET_TOKEN']
[12:25:04]   Plugin: github:jpshackelford/oh-examples / per-conversation-secrets/test-plugin
[12:25:04]   Secrets in response: {'MCP_SERVER_URL': '**********', 'MCP_SECRET_TOKEN': '**********'}
[12:25:07]   Conversation ID: da3e42ff-6d52-4617-a514-023b9b90a751
[12:25:37] Asking agent to call validate_token MCP tool...
[12:26:37] Checking events for MCP validation result...
[12:26:37]   Total events: 15

======================================================================
  ✅ SUCCESS! MCP secrets-at-start expansion works!

  Proven workflow:
  1. Conversation started with secrets AND plugin in same request
  2. Secrets: MCP_SERVER_URL and MCP_SECRET_TOKEN
  3. Plugin loaded with MCP config containing ${MCP_SERVER_URL} and ${MCP_SECRET_TOKEN}
  4. Both variables expanded from secrets passed at conversation start
  5. MCP server received correct URL and token
  6. Token validation succeeded!

  Match found: ✅ SUCCESS! Token validated correctly. Validation count: 1
======================================================================
```

#### MCP Server Logs Confirm Token Received
```
[12:25:07] POST /mcp session=b51e1bd1-adf3-4616-8eab-8adc9b2a2fe4
[12:25:07]   Headers: Authorization=Bearer per-conv-secret-xyz-123...
[12:25:39] Validating token: 'per-conv-secret-xyz-123' vs expected: 'per-conv-secret-xyz-123'
```

### Key Observations

1. **MCP variable expansion works**: `${MCP_SERVER_URL}` and `${MCP_SECRET_TOKEN}` in the plugin's `.mcp.json` were correctly expanded from secrets passed at conversation start
2. **Both URL and auth token expanded**: Not just the token, but also the server URL was parameterized and expanded
3. **Token received by MCP server**: The server logs confirm the correct token was passed in the `Authorization` header
4. **No script modifications needed**: The test worked on the first run after updating the plugin to use variables

---

### How to Reproduce

```bash
# Terminal 1: Start the MCP server
cd oh-examples/per-conversation-secrets
python mcp_server.py --port 12000 --expected-token "per-conv-secret-xyz-123"

# Terminal 2: Run the test
export OH_API_KEY="<your-api-key>"
export OH_API_URL="https://ohpr-14009-30.staging.all-hands.dev/api"
export MCP_SERVER_URL="https://work-1-xxx.prod-runtime.all-hands.dev"  # where mcp_server.py is accessible

python test_mcp_secrets_at_start.py
```

---

### Summary of All Tests

| Test | What It Verifies | Result |
|------|------------------|--------|
| `test_secrets_at_start.py` | Secrets available as env vars in bash | ✅ PASSED |
| `test_secrets.py` | Secrets injected after start (backward compat) | ✅ PASSED |
| `test_mcp_secrets_at_start.py` | **MCP config variable expansion from secrets at start** | ✅ PASSED |

All three tests pass, confirming PR #14009 + SDK #2873 work correctly for both bash environment variables and MCP config variable expansion.

feedback.author: jpshackelford
feedback.body:
## 🔐 Secret Handling Flow - Primer

To help clarify the security model for secrets passed via the new `secrets` field, here's a walkthrough of how secrets flow through the system:

### 1. API Request (App Server)

```
POST /v1/app-conversations
{
  "secrets": {"MY_API_KEY": "actual-secret-value", ...}
}
```

The `secrets` field uses `dict[str, SecretStr]`. Pydantic's `SecretStr` **automatically masks** values as `**********` in any serialization (logs, `str()`, `repr()`, JSON) unless explicitly exposed.

### 2. Task DB Storage (App Server)

The `AppConversationStartTask` is persisted for status tracking:

```python
# Stored in DB as:
{"secrets": {"MY_API_KEY": "**********"}}
```

This is **intentional** - the task record is an audit trail showing *which* secret names were provided, without exposing values. Secrets are used immediately from the in-memory request object, never retrieved from DB.

### 3. Agent Server Transmission

Before sending to the agent server, `SecretStr` values are converted to SDK's `StaticSecret`:

```python
secrets[name] = StaticSecret(value=value)  # line 1259
```

The `StartConversationRequest` is serialized with `context={'expose_secrets': True}` (line 323), which the SDK's `StaticSecret` serializer respects - exposing plaintext for secure transmission to the agent server.

### 4. SDK Persistence (Agent Server)

Once in the agent server, secrets live in `ConversationState.secret_registry`. The SDK has its own persistence model:

- **With `cipher`**: Secrets are **encrypted** using Fernet symmetric encryption
- **Without `cipher`**: Secrets are **redacted** (`**********`) with a warning log

```python
# From SDK conversation/state.py
if not self._cipher and self.secret_registry.secret_sources:
    logger.warning(
        f"Saving conversation state without cipher - "
        f"{len(self.secret_registry.secret_sources)} secret(s) will be "
        "redacted and lost on restore."
    )
```

### Summary

| Location | Storage Format | Purpose |
|----------|---------------|---------|
| App Server Task DB | `**********` (masked) | Audit trail of secret names |
| Agent Server transmission | Plaintext (via `expose_secrets`) | Secure internal API call |
| SDK ConversationState | Encrypted (with cipher) or redacted | Conversation persistence |

The `SecretStr` type in this PR provides defense-in-depth - even if accidentally logged or serialized without the proper context, values are masked.

---
*This comment was written by an AI assistant (OpenHands) to help document the security model.*

feedback.author: jpshackelford
feedback.body:
@tofarr - I did a deep dive into your review comment about adding cipher logic. Here's my analysis:

## Deep Dive Analysis: Secret Handling in PR #14009

### Your Comment
> "This represents a change to the sensitivity of AppConversationStartRequest - it now contains sensitive data. Therefore rather than expose secrets True, we should add a custom serializer and apply the same cipher logic from the SDK (And supply a cipher instead)"

---

## PART A: Does the Solution Protect from Any Exposure of Secrets?

### ✅ **YES - The current solution IS sufficient for secret protection**

**Key Finding**: The `secrets` field uses Pydantic's built-in `SecretStr`:
```python
secrets: dict[str, SecretStr] | None = Field(...)
```

**Why this is safe**:

1. **Standard `SecretStr` does NOT respond to `expose_secrets` context**
   - I verified this empirically:
   ```
   === Standard SecretStr (what PR uses) ===
     Default: {'secrets': {'KEY': '**********'}}
     expose_secrets=True: {'secrets': {'KEY': '**********'}}  ← STILL REDACTED!
   ```

2. **This differs from SDK's `StaticSecret`** which has a custom serializer:
   ```
   === Custom Serializer (like SDK StaticSecret) ===  
     Default: {'value': '**********'}
     expose_secrets=True: {'value': 'my-secret-value'}  ← EXPOSED
   ```

3. **SQL Storage Path** (Task DB):
   - `create_json_type_decorator` uses `expose_secrets=True` (sql_utils.py:35)
   - BUT since the field is `dict[str, SecretStr]`, secrets are stored as `{"MY_KEY": "**********"}`
   - **Result**: No plaintext secrets leak to the database

4. **Agent Server Transmission**:
   - Before sending to agent server, secrets are converted to `StaticSecret` (line 1262):
     ```python
     secrets[name] = StaticSecret(value=value)  # Now CAN be exposed
     ```
   - The `StartConversationRequest` is serialized with `expose_secrets=True`
   - `StaticSecret` DOES respond to this context, so plaintext secrets are correctly transmitted
   - **Result**: Agent server receives the actual secret values

**Conclusion for Part A**: The `SecretStr` type provides automatic protection that does NOT require cipher encryption. The suggestion to add cipher logic like the SDK would be redundant - the secrets are already safely redacted in stored form.

---

## PART B: Does the Solution Allow for Sandbox Pause and Resume with Secrets Available?

### ✅ **YES - Secrets persist across pause/resume** (with standard configuration)

**How it works**:

1. **Secrets flow to agent-server**:
   ```
   AppConversationStartRequest.secrets (SecretStr - redacted in storage)
        ↓ converted to
   StaticSecret (exposed for transmission)
        ↓ sent via
   StartConversationRequest to agent-server
        ↓ stored in
   ConversationState.secret_registry
   ```

2. **Agent-server cipher configuration** (config.py lines 195-207):
   ```python
   @property
   def cipher(self) -> Cipher | None:
       if self.secret_key is None:
           logger.warning("⚠️ OH_SECRET_KEY was not defined. Secrets will not be persisted...")
           return None
       return Cipher(self.secret_key.get_secret_value())
   ```
   - If `OH_SECRET_KEY` is set → cipher is created
   - Falls back to `SESSION_API_KEY` (always set in production)
   - If neither set → secrets are redacted on persistence

3. **ConversationState persistence** (state.py lines 239-256):
   ```python
   def _save_base_state(self, fs: FileStore) -> None:
       context = {"cipher": self._cipher} if self._cipher else None
       if not self._cipher and self.secret_registry.secret_sources:
           logger.warning("...secrets will be redacted and lost on restore.")
       payload = self.model_dump_json(exclude_none=True, context=context)
   ```

4. **Pause/Resume flow**:
   - **Pause**: Pod stopped, PVC persisted, ConversationState saved with encrypted secrets
   - **Resume**: PVC restored, `ConversationState.create()` loads and decrypts secrets
   - Cipher passed through: `EventService(cipher=self.cipher)` → `LocalConversation(cipher=cipher)` → `ConversationState.create(cipher=cipher)`

**Production guarantee**: In OpenHands Cloud, `SESSION_API_KEY` is always provided by the app-server to the runtime, so a cipher is always available for secret persistence.

---

## Summary

| Aspect | Your Concern | Actual Situation |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Secret exposure in Task DB | Suggested adding cipher encryption | **Unnecessary** - `SecretStr` already redacts to `**********` regardless of `expose_secrets` context |
| Secret transmission to agent-server | N/A | **Works correctly** - Conversion to `StaticSecret` enables proper exposure for transmission |
| Pause/Resume persistence | Suggested cipher logic | **Already implemented** - Agent-server uses cipher for `ConversationState` persistence |

### Verdict: The current solution is **sufficient and correct**.

The only improvement I'd suggest is adding a code comment explaining why `SecretStr` is safe here (because unlike `StaticSecret`, it doesn't respond to `expose_secrets` context), which could help prevent future confusion.

---
_This analysis was performed by an AI assistant (OpenHands) on behalf of @jpshackelford._

problem:
refactor(server): extract legacy HTTP router registration from app.py

### Is there an existing feature request for this?

- [x] I have searched existing issues and feature requests, and this is not a duplicate.

### Problem or Use Case

linked_prs.title: refactor(server): extract legacy HTTP routers into legacy_http_routes…

solution.text:
PR #14026: refactor(server): extract legacy HTTP routers into legacy_http_routes…

### Summary
Moves legacy (V0) app.include_router(...) wiring out of openhands/server/app.py into openhands/server/legacy_http_routes.py, behind a single registrar register_legacy_http_routes(app, server_config=...). app.py stays focused on FastAPI construction, lifespan, MCP mount, and the auth exception handler.

Closes https://github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands/issues/14023

### Motivation

  - Makes the deprecated HTTP boundary obvious in one module.
  - Shortens app.py so app-level wiring is easier to read.
  - Sets up a future, reviewable change to sunset V0 routes without mixing that policy work into this refactor.

### What changed

  - Added openhands/server/legacy_http_routes.py with module/function docstrings that state V0/legacy 
    registration and point new HTTP work at openhands/app_server/README.md.
  - Updated openhands/server/app.py to import and call register_legacy_http_routes instead of inlining router
    imports and include_router calls.

### Behavior / parity

  - No intentional behavior change: same routes for the same config/environment as before.
  - Order and guards preserved: router order matches the pre-refactor tree; git router still only 
    when server_config.app_mode == AppMode.OPENHANDS; V1 router still only when server_config.enable_v1.

### Out of scope

  - Removing V0 routes or changing OpenAPI deprecation metadata.
  - Changing ENABLE_V1 / server_config.enable_v1 semantics.

### How to review

  - Confirm the diff is mostly moves: compare include_router sequence and conditionals to the previous app.py.
  - Sanity-check that only openhands/server/ call sites needed updates (if any).

### Testing

  -  Existing server / integration tests and CI pass
  -  [Optional] Smoke: start server and hit a representative legacy + health endpoint

Changed files: .pre-commit-cache/.lock, .pre-commit-cache/README, .pre-commit-cache/db.db, .pre-commit-cache/pre-commit.log, frontend/README.md, openhands/server/app.py, openhands/server/legacy_http_routes.py
